subject: Physics
q: Nearly <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the power of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>110</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> light bulb is converted to visible radiation. The change in average intensities of visible radiation, at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the bulb to a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>' will be
question: Nearly <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the power of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>110</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> light bulb is converted to visible radiation. The change in average intensities of visible radiation, at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the bulb to a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math>' will be
solution:
Power of visible radiation is P'=10100×110 W =11 W

Change in average intensity of visible radiation is  ∆Iradiation=Iradiation1-Iradiation2

⇒I1-I2=P'4πr12-P'4πr22

=114π11-125  =114π×2425

=264π×10-2=84×10-2 W m-2

Hence, the value of a=84.

subject: Physics
q: The temperature of a body in air falls from $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $24^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in 4 minutes. The temperature of the air is $16^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The temperature of the body in the next 4 minutes will be:
question: The temperature of a body in air falls from $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $24^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in 4 minutes. The temperature of the air is $16^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The temperature of the body in the next 4 minutes will be:
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \Delta T=T_2-T_1=16^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\
& \text { And } T_0=16^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\
& \frac{\Delta T}{t_1}=-k\left(32-16^{\circ}\right)...(i) \\
& \frac{\left(24-T_3\right)}{4}=-k\left(\frac{24+T_3}{2}-16\right) ....(ii) \\
& \frac{16}{4}=-k(16) \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{\left(24-T_3\right)}{4}=-k\left(12+\frac{T_3}{2}-16\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{16}{24-T_3}=\frac{16}{T_3} T_2 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{T_3}{2}-4=24-T_3 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{3 T_3}{2}=28 \\
& \Rightarrow T_3=\frac{56}{3}{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: On a linear temperature scale $\mathrm{Y}$, water freezes at $-160^{\circ} \mathrm{Y}$ and boils at $-50^{\circ} \mathrm{Y}$. On this $\mathrm{Y}$ scale, a temperature of $340 \mathrm{~K}$ would be read as : (water freezes at $273 \mathrm{~K}$ and boils at $373 \mathrm{~K}$ )
question: On a linear temperature scale $\mathrm{Y}$, water freezes at $-160^{\circ} \mathrm{Y}$ and boils at $-50^{\circ} \mathrm{Y}$. On this $\mathrm{Y}$ scale, a temperature of $340 \mathrm{~K}$ would be read as : (water freezes at $273 \mathrm{~K}$ and boils at $373 \mathrm{~K}$ )
solution:
Reading on any scale - LFP
UFP - LFP
$=$ constant for all scales
$\frac{340-273}{373-273}=\frac{{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{y}-(-160)}{-50-(-160)}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{67}{100}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+160}{110}$
$\therefore \quad y=-86.3^{\circ} \mathrm{y}$

subject: Physics
q: A solid metallic cube having total surface area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is uniformly heated. If its temperature is increased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, calculate the increase in volume of the cube. (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mo>∘</mo></mmultiscripts><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>).
question: A solid metallic cube having total surface area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is uniformly heated. If its temperature is increased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, calculate the increase in volume of the cube. (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mo>∘</mo></mmultiscripts><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>).
solution:
Total surface area of a cube is 6a2=24 m2⇒a2=4 ⇒a=2 m

Volume of a cube V=a3=8 m3

Change in volume is given by, 

ΔV=V0γΔT, where γ=3α

ΔV=8×1.5×10-4×10 m3⇒ΔV=1.2×105 cm3

subject: Physics
q:
10 kg of ice at $-10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is added to 100 kg of water to lower its temperature from 25 ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Consider no heat exchange to surroundings. The decrement to the temperature of water is $\_\_\_\_$ ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
(specific heat of ice $=2100 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{Kg}.{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, specific heat of water $=4200 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{Kg}.{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, latent heat of fusion of ice $=3.36 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{Kg}$)
question:
10 kg of ice at $-10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is added to 100 kg of water to lower its temperature from 25 ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Consider no heat exchange to surroundings. The decrement to the temperature of water is $\_\_\_\_$ ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
(specific heat of ice $=2100 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{Kg}.{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, specific heat of water $=4200 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{Kg}.{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, latent heat of fusion of ice $=3.36 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{Kg}$)
solution:
Heat available from water cooling 25°C to 0°C: $Q_{available} = 100 \times 4200 \times 25 = 10.5 \times 10^6$ J.
Heat to warm ice from -10°C to 0°C: $Q_1 = 10 \times 2100 \times 10 = 0.21 \times 10^6$ J.
Heat to melt ice at 0°C: $Q_2 = 10 \times 3.36 \times 10^5 = 3.36 \times 10^6$ J.
Total needed: $0.21 + 3.36 = 3.57 \times 10^6$ J.
Since available heat exceeds required heat, all ice melts. Remaining heat: $10.5 - 3.57 = 6.93 \times 10^6$ J
warms 110 kg water: $\Delta T = \frac{6.93 \times 10^6}{110 \times 4200} = 15$ °C.
Final temperature: 15°C.
Decrement in water temperature: $25 - 15 = 10$ °C.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : When a rod lying freely is heated, no thermal stress is developed in it.

Reason R : On heating, the length of the rod increases.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : When a rod lying freely is heated, no thermal stress is developed in it.

Reason R : On heating, the length of the rod increases.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are true and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are true but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is NOT the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>
solution:
Thermal stress is mechanical stress created by any change in temperature of a material. When the body is under some constraints, thermal stress is generated. But if it is lying freely, no thermal stress is developed.

The increase in length of a body with temperature is given by

∆L=Lα∆T

So, with the increase in temperature, the length of the rod increases.

A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

subject: Physics
q:
A black coloured solid sphere of radius R and mass M is inside a cavity with a vacuum inside. The walls of the cavity are maintained at temperature T0. The initial temperature of the sphere is 3T0. If the specific heat of the material of the sphere varies as αT3 per unit mass with the temperature T of the sphere, where α is a constant, then the time taken for the sphere to cool down to temperature 2T0 will be

(σ is Stefan Boltzmann constant)
question:
A black coloured solid sphere of radius R and mass M is inside a cavity with a vacuum inside. The walls of the cavity are maintained at temperature T0. The initial temperature of the sphere is 3T0. If the specific heat of the material of the sphere varies as αT3 per unit mass with the temperature T of the sphere, where α is a constant, then the time taken for the sphere to cool down to temperature 2T0 will be

(σ is Stefan Boltzmann constant)
solution:
dθdt=-σAeMsθ4-θ04      



dθdt=-σAeMαθ3θ4-θ04

∫θ3θ4-θ04dθ=-σAeMα∫dt

Let θ4-θ04=x

4θ3dθ=dx

∫dx4x=-σAeMαt0t

14lnx=MσAeMαt0t

14lnθ4-θ042T03T0=-σAeMαt

14ln2T04-T043T04-T04=-σAeMαt

t=-αM4σAeln15T0480T04

=-αM4σAeln316

=-αM4σ4πR2eln316         where e=1

t=αM16πσR2ln163

subject: Physics
q: A heat source at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> is connected to another heat reservoir at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> by a copper slab which is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> thick. Given that the thermal conductivity of copper is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.1</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the energy flux through it in the steady-state is:
question: A heat source at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> is connected to another heat reservoir at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> by a copper slab which is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> thick. Given that the thermal conductivity of copper is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.1</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the energy flux through it in the steady-state is:
solution:
Given,

Temperature of heat source, TH=103 K,

Temperature of heat reservoir, TL=102 K,

Conductivity of copper K=0.1 W K-1m-1

From the equation of steady-state heat flow,

dQdt=KA∆TL

Heat energy flux can be defined as the rate of heat energy transfer through a given surface.

1AdQdt=K×1000-1001=90 W m-2

subject: Physics
q:
A lead bullet penetrates into a solid object and melts. Assuming that 40% of its kinetic energy is used to heat it, the initial speed of bullet is
(Given, initial temperature of the bullet =127°C,
Melting point of the bullet =327°C,
Latent heat of fusion of lead =2.5×104 J kg-1,
Specific heat capacity of lead =125 J kg K-1)
question:
A lead bullet penetrates into a solid object and melts. Assuming that 40% of its kinetic energy is used to heat it, the initial speed of bullet is
(Given, initial temperature of the bullet =127°C,
Melting point of the bullet =327°C,
Latent heat of fusion of lead =2.5×104 J kg-1,
Specific heat capacity of lead =125 J kg K-1)
solution:
Loss of kinetic energy of the lead bullet=Heat generated to raise its temperature from 127 °C to 327 °C.

40 % loss of the kinetic energy of the lead bullet=Heat gained by the lead bullet to raise its temperature.

0.412mv2=m×SPb×∆T+m×LPb

0.4×12mv2=mSPbT2-t1+mLPb

0.2mv2=m×125×327-127+2.5×104

v2=5×125×200+2.5×104

v2=5×25000+25000

v=500 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: A faulty thermometer reads <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> in melting ice and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>95</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> in steam. The correct temperature on absolute scale will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> when the faulty thermometer reads <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>41</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>.
question: A faulty thermometer reads <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> in melting ice and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>95</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> in steam. The correct temperature on absolute scale will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> when the faulty thermometer reads <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>41</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>.
solution:
As per working of thermometers, X-LFPUFP-LFP=constant.

Therefore, we can write

 41°-5°95°-5°=C-0°100°-0°

⇒C=3690×100=40°C=40+273 K=313 K

subject: Physics
q: When the temperature of a metal wire is increased from 0ºC to 10ºC, its length increases by 0.02%.The percentage change in its mass density will be closed to:
question: When the temperature of a metal wire is increased from 0ºC to 10ºC, its length increases by 0.02%.The percentage change in its mass density will be closed to:
solution:
Δℓ=ℓαΔt
α=ΔℓℓΔT=0.02100×10;  α=2×10-5
γ=3α=6×10-5
ΔVV=γ×ΔT;  ΔVV×100=6×10-5×10×100=6×10-2
Volume increase by 0.06% therefore density decrease by 0.06%

subject: Physics
q: The temperature of a metal strip having coefficient of linear expansion $\alpha$ is increased from $T_1$ to $T_2$ resulting in increase of its length by $\Delta L_1$. The temperature is further increased from $T_2$ to $T_3$ such that the increase in its length is $\Delta L_2$. Given $T_3+T_1=2T_2$ and $T_2-T_1=\Delta T$, the value of $\Delta L_2$ is ______.
question: The temperature of a metal strip having coefficient of linear expansion $\alpha$ is increased from $T_1$ to $T_2$ resulting in increase of its length by $\Delta L_1$. The temperature is further increased from $T_2$ to $T_3$ such that the increase in its length is $\Delta L_2$. Given $T_3+T_1=2T_2$ and $T_2-T_1=\Delta T$, the value of $\Delta L_2$ is ______.
solution:
Let the length of the metal strip at temperature $T_1$ be $L_1$.

When the temperature is increased from $T_1$ to $T_2$, the new length $L_2$ is given by:

$L_2 = L_1(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$

where $\Delta T = T_2 - T_1$.

The increase in length is:

$\Delta L_1 = L_2 - L_1 = L_1 \alpha \Delta T$

When the temperature is further increased from $T_2$ to $T_3$, the new length $L_3$ is:

$L_3 = L_2(1 + \alpha \Delta T')$

where $\Delta T' = T_3 - T_2$.

Given $T_3 + T_1 = 2T_2$, we have $T_3 - T_2 = T_2 - T_1 = \Delta T$.

Thus, $\Delta T' = \Delta T$.

The increase in length for this second temperature change is:

$\Delta L_2 = L_3 - L_2 = L_2 \alpha \Delta T$

Substituting $L_2 = L_1(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ into the equation for $\Delta L_2$:

$\Delta L_2 = L_1(1 + \alpha \Delta T) \alpha \Delta T$

$\Delta L_2 = (L_1 \alpha \Delta T)(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$

Since $\Delta L_1 = L_1 \alpha \Delta T$, substituting this gives:

$\Delta L_2 = \Delta L_1(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$

Answer: $\Delta L_1[1+\alpha\Delta T]$

subject: Physics
q: A steel rod of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is heated from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> without being allowed to extend or bend. The compressive tension produced in the rod is _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. (Given Young's modulus of steel <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, coefficient of linear expansion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> )
question: A steel rod of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is heated from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> without being allowed to extend or bend. The compressive tension produced in the rod is _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. (Given Young's modulus of steel <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, coefficient of linear expansion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> )
solution:
The formula to calculate the Young's modulus of the material of the wire is given by

Y=FLA∆L   ...1

The increase in length of the wire due to increase in temperature is given by

∆L=LαT2-T1   ...2

Substitute the expression for the extension in length from equation (2) into equation (1) and simplify to obtain the required compressive tension.

Y=FLALαT2-T1⇒F=AαYT2-T1   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required compressive tension in the wire.

F=10-4 m2×10-5 K-1×2×1011 Nm-2×200° C-0° C= 4×104 N

subject: Physics
q: Hot water cools from $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in the first 10 minutes and to $42^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in the next 10 minutes. The temperature of the surroundings is:
question: Hot water cools from $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in the first 10 minutes and to $42^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in the next 10 minutes. The temperature of the surroundings is:
solution:
By Newton's law of cooling
$$
\frac{\theta_1-\theta_2}{\mathrm{t}}=-\mathrm{K}\left[\frac{\theta_1+\theta_2}{2}-\theta_0\right]
$$
where $\theta_0$ is the temperature of surrounding. Now, hot water cools from $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in 10 minutes,
$$
\frac{60-50}{10}=-K\left[\frac{60+50}{2}-\theta_0\right]
$$
Again, it cools from $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $42^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in next 10 minutes.
$$
\frac{50-42}{10}=-\mathrm{K}\left[\frac{50+42}{2}-\theta_0\right]
$$
Dividing equations (i) by (ii) we get

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{1}{0.8}=\frac{55-\theta_0}{46-\theta_0} \\
&\frac{10}{8}=\frac{55-\theta_0}{46-\theta_0} \\
&460-10 \theta_0=440-8 \theta_0 \\
&2 \theta_0=20 \\
&\theta_0=10^{\circ} \mathrm{c}
\end{aligned}
$$

q: A container is divided into two chambers by a partition. The volume of first chamber is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>litre</mi></math> and second chamber is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>litre</mi></math>. The first chamber contain <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math> moles of gas at pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math> and second chamber contain <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math> moles of gas at pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math>. After the partition is removed and the mixture attains equilibrium, then, the common equilibrium pressure existing in the mixture is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math>. Value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> (nearest integer) is <u>             </u>
question: A container is divided into two chambers by a partition. The volume of first chamber is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>litre</mi></math> and second chamber is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>litre</mi></math>. The first chamber contain <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math> moles of gas at pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math> and second chamber contain <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math> moles of gas at pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math>. After the partition is removed and the mixture attains equilibrium, then, the common equilibrium pressure existing in the mixture is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math>. Value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> (nearest integer) is <u>             </u>
solution:
Let common equilibrium pressure of mixture is P atmp. then

f2P1 V1+f2P2 V2=f2PV1+V2

f224.5+f235.5=f2P4.5+5.5

⇒P=2.55=x×10-1 atmp

So x=25.5≈26 (Nearest integer)

Area of cross-section of each rod is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. End of copper rod is maintained at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Where as ends of brass and steel are kept at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Lengths of the copper, brass and steel rods are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>46</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cms</mi></math> respectively. The rods are thermally insulated from surroundings except at ends. Thermal conductivities of copper, brass and steel are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>92</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>26</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>12</mn></math> CGS units respectively. Rate of heat flow through copper rod is :
question: Three rods of Copper, Brass and Steel are welded together to form a Y-shaped structure. Area of cross-section of each rod is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. End of copper rod is maintained at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Where as ends of brass and steel are kept at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Lengths of the copper, brass and steel rods are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>46</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cms</mi></math> respectively. The rods are thermally insulated from surroundings except at ends. Thermal conductivities of copper, brass and steel are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>92</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>26</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>12</mn></math> CGS units respectively. Rate of heat flow through copper rod is :
solution:
LCu = 46 cm; LB = 13 cm & LS=12 cm.



KCu=0.92KB=0.26KS=0.12}CGSunit

We know that R=LKA

RCu=460.92×4=12.5

RB=130.26×4=12.5

RS=120.12×4=25

Now, if T is temperature of junction then

  100-TRCu=T-0RB+T-0RS

  100-T12.5=T12.5+T25

  ∴100-T12.5=3T25

  200=5T

  ∴T=40∘

⇒QCu=100-TRCu

              =100-4012.5

              =6012.5

  ⇒QCu=4.8 Cal/s

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment to verify Newton's law of cooling. a graph is plotted between. the temperature difference ΔT of the water and surroundings and time as shown in figure. The initial temperature of water is taken as 80°C. The value of t2 as mentioned in the graph will be
question: In an experiment to verify Newton's law of cooling. a graph is plotted between. the temperature difference ΔT of the water and surroundings and time as shown in figure. The initial temperature of water is taken as 80°C. The value of t2 as mentioned in the graph will be
solution:
According to the Newton's law of cooling, -dTdt=KT-T0.

Applying approximation, -∆T∆t=KT1+T22-T0 

For first 6 min, T1=80°C, T2=40+20=60°C, T0=20°C

⇒-60-806=K70-20⇒K=-60-806×70-20

⇒K=206×50=115

For 6 to t2, T1=60°C, T2=20+20=40°C, T0=20°C

-60-80t2-6=K50-20⇒t2-6=15×2030⇒t2=16 min

subject: Physics
q:
A mass of $50 \mathrm{~g}$ of water in a closed vessel, with surroundings at a constant temperature takes 2 minutes to cool from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. A mass of $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of another liquid in an identical vessel with identical surroundings takes the same time to cool from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The specific heat of the liquid is :
(The water equivalent of the vessel is $30 \mathrm{~g}$.)
question:
A mass of $50 \mathrm{~g}$ of water in a closed vessel, with surroundings at a constant temperature takes 2 minutes to cool from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. A mass of $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of another liquid in an identical vessel with identical surroundings takes the same time to cool from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The specific heat of the liquid is :
(The water equivalent of the vessel is $30 \mathrm{~g}$.)
solution:
As the surrounding is identical, vessel is identical time taken to cool both water and liquid (from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) is same 2 minutes, therefore
$$
\left(\frac{\mathrm{dQ}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{\text {water }}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{dQ}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{\text {liquid }}
$$
or, $\frac{\left(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{W}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{W}}+\mathrm{W}\right) \Delta \mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{t}}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{m}_{\ell} \mathrm{C}_{\ell}+\mathrm{W}\right) \Delta \mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{t}}$
$(\mathrm{W}=$ water equivalent of the vessel $)$
or, $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{w}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{w}}=\mathrm{m}_{\ell} \mathrm{C}_{\ell}$
$\therefore$ Specific heat of liquid, $\mathrm{C}_{\ell}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{W}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{W}}}{\mathrm{m}_{\ell}}$
$$
=\frac{50 \times 1}{100}=0.5 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{kg}
$$

question: A bimetallic strip consists of metals A and B. It is mounted rigidly as shown. The metal A has higher coefficient of expansion compared to that of metal B. When the bimetallic strip is placed in a cold both, it will :
solution:
αA>αB

Length of both strips will decrease ΔLA>ΔLB

subject: Physics
q: Two conductors have the same resistance at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ but their temperature coefficients of resistance are $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$. The respective temperature coefficients of their series and parallel combinations are nearly
question: Two conductors have the same resistance at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ but their temperature coefficients of resistance are $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$. The respective temperature coefficients of their series and parallel combinations are nearly
solution:
Let $R_0$ be the initial resistance of both conductors $\therefore \quad$ At temperature $\theta$ their resistance will be, $R_1=R_0\left(1+\alpha_1 \theta\right)$ and $R_2=R_0\left(1+\alpha_2 \theta\right)$
for, series combination, $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{s}}=\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2$ $R_{s 0}\left(1+\alpha_s \theta\right)=R_0\left(1+\alpha_1 \theta\right)+R_0\left(1+\alpha_2 \theta\right)$
where $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{s} 0}=\mathrm{R}_0+\mathrm{R}_0=2 \mathrm{R}_0$
$\therefore \quad 2 \mathrm{R}_0\left(1+\alpha_s \theta\right)=2 \mathrm{R}_0+\mathrm{R}_0 \theta\left(\alpha_1+\alpha_2\right)$
or $\quad \alpha_s=\frac{\alpha_1+\alpha_2}{2}$
for parallel combination,
$$
R_p=\frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2}
$$
where, $R_{p 0}=\frac{R_0 R_0}{R_0+R_0}=\frac{R_0}{2}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{R}_0}{2}\left(1+\alpha_{\mathrm{p}} \theta\right)=\frac{\mathrm{R}_0^2\left(1+\alpha_1 \theta+\alpha_2 \theta+\alpha_1 \alpha_2 \theta\right)}{\mathrm{R}_0\left(2+\alpha_1 \theta+\alpha_2 \theta\right)}$
as $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ are small quantities
$\therefore \quad \alpha_1 \alpha_2$ is negligible
or $\quad \alpha_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{\alpha_1+\alpha_2}{2+\left(\alpha_1+\alpha_2\right) \theta}=\frac{\alpha_1+\alpha_2}{2}\left[1-\left(\alpha_1+\alpha_2\right) \theta\right]$
as $\left(\alpha_1+\alpha_2\right)^2$ is negligible
$\therefore \quad \alpha_p=\frac{\alpha_1+\alpha_2}{2}$

subject: Physics
q: On a temperature scale '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>', the boiling point of water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>65</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> and the freezing point is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>15</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>. Assuming that the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> scale is linear. The equivalent temperature corresponding to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>95</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> on the Fahrenheit scale would be
question: On a temperature scale '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>', the boiling point of water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>65</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> and the freezing point is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>15</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>. Assuming that the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> scale is linear. The equivalent temperature corresponding to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>95</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> on the Fahrenheit scale would be
solution:
The relation between two temperature scales can be written as follows

65-x65--15=212-F180   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters in to equation (1) and sole to calculate the required temperature in Fahrenheit scale.

65+9580=212-F180⇒2×180=212-F⇒F=212-360=-148°F

subject: Physics
q:
A water heater of power 2000 W is used to heat water. The specific heat capacity of water is 4200 J kg-1 K-1. The efficiency of heater is 70%. Time required to heat 2 kg of water from 10°C to 60°C is _____ s.

(Assume that the specific heat capacity of water remains constant over the temperature range of the water).
question:
A water heater of power 2000 W is used to heat water. The specific heat capacity of water is 4200 J kg-1 K-1. The efficiency of heater is 70%. Time required to heat 2 kg of water from 10°C to 60°C is _____ s.

(Assume that the specific heat capacity of water remains constant over the temperature range of the water).
solution:
Heat provided to water will be responsible for increase in temperature. Therefore, we can write

η×P×t=M×s×ΔT

Now, η=70100=0.7 and energy=power×time.

Therefore,

t=2×4200×60°-10°0.7×2000 s=300 s

subject: Physics
q: If a piece of metal is heated to temperature<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mi>θ</mi></math> and then allowed to cool in a room which is at temperature<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>, the graph between the temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> of the metal and time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> will be closest to :
question: If a piece of metal is heated to temperature<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mi>θ</mi></math> and then allowed to cool in a room which is at temperature<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>, the graph between the temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> of the metal and time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> will be closest to :
solution:
According to Newton's cooling law, rate of cooling is

dθdt∝θ-θ0

Here, θ=the temperature of the body

θ0=Room temperature(constant)

Let k be the proportionality constant and θi be the initial temperature.
Therefore, 

dθdt=-kθ-θ0⇒∫dθθ-θ0=-k∫dt⇒lnθ-θ0=-kt⇒θ=θ0+θ-θ0e-kt

Hence, the graph is exponential.

q: A uniform cylindrical rod of length <math><mi>L</mi></math> and radius r, is made from a material whose Young’s modulus of Elasticity equals <math><mi>Y</mi><mo>.</mo></math> When this rod is heated by temperature T and simultaneously subjected to a net longutudinal compressional force F, its length remains unchanged. The coefficient of volume expansion, of the material of the rod, is (nearly) equal to:
question: A uniform cylindrical rod of length <math><mi>L</mi></math> and radius r, is made from a material whose Young’s modulus of Elasticity equals <math><mi>Y</mi><mo>.</mo></math> When this rod is heated by temperature T and simultaneously subjected to a net longutudinal compressional force F, its length remains unchanged. The coefficient of volume expansion, of the material of the rod, is (nearly) equal to:
solution:
Stress =FA
YαT=FA
α=FAYT
γ=3α=3FAYT
γ=3Fπr2YT

subject: Physics
q: Two spherical bodies of same materials having radii 0.2 m and 0.8 m are placed in same atmosphere. The temperature of the smaller body is 800 K and temperature of the bigger body is 400 K . If the energy radiated from the smaller body is E, the energy radiated from the bigger body is (assume, effect of the surrounding temperature to be negligible),
question: Two spherical bodies of same materials having radii 0.2 m and 0.8 m are placed in same atmosphere. The temperature of the smaller body is 800 K and temperature of the bigger body is 400 K . If the energy radiated from the smaller body is E, the energy radiated from the bigger body is (assume, effect of the surrounding temperature to be negligible),
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}}=\sigma \mathrm{eAT}^4 \Rightarrow \mathrm{P} \propto \mathrm{AT}^4 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{P}_{\text {smaller }}}{\mathrm{P}_{\text {larger }}}=\frac{(0.2)^2 \times 800^4}{(0.8)^2 \times 400^4} \\ & \frac{1}{16} \times 16=1 \\ & \therefore \mathrm{P}_{\text {larger }}=\mathrm{P}_{\text {smaller }}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A bowl filled with very hot soup cools from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>98</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to $86^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in 2 minutes when the room temperature is $22^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. How long it will take to cool from $75^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $69^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
question: A bowl filled with very hot soup cools from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>98</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to $86^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in 2 minutes when the room temperature is $22^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. How long it will take to cool from $75^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $69^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
solution:
From Newton's law of cooling, we have

ΔQΔt=-KTavg-T0

Where, Q=ms∆T, m is the mass, s is the specific heat and ∆T is the temperature change.

Case 1:

∆t=2 minutes, T0=22°C

ms×122=-K98+862-22

6=-Kms70    ...1

Case 2:

ms×6Δt=-K75+692-22

6Δt=-Kms(50)   ...2

Dividing (2) by (1), we get

⇒6Δt(6)=5070

⇒Δt=75=1.4 min

q: On celcius scale the temperature of body increases by $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The increase in temperature on Fahrenheit scale is :
question: On celcius scale the temperature of body increases by $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The increase in temperature on Fahrenheit scale is :
solution:
We know that per ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ change is equivalent to $1.8^{\circ}$ change in ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$.
$\therefore 40^{\circ}$ change on celcius scale will corresponds to $72^{\circ}$ change on Fahrenheit scale.
Hence option (3)

q: If K1 and K2 are the thermal conductivities L1 and L2 are the lengths and A1 and A2 are the cross sectional areas of steel and copper rods respectively such that K2K1=9,A1A2=2,L1L2=2. Then, for the arrangement as shown in the figure. The value of temperature T of the steel - copper junction in the steady state will be
question: If K1 and K2 are the thermal conductivities L1 and L2 are the lengths and A1 and A2 are the cross sectional areas of steel and copper rods respectively such that K2K1=9,A1A2=2,L1L2=2. Then, for the arrangement as shown in the figure. The value of temperature T of the steel - copper junction in the steady state will be
solution:
At steady state, the heat conducted by both the rods will be the same.

The rate of heat conduction is given by, dQdt=KAl∆T. Therefore, if the temperature of the junction is T,

K1 A1l1T1-T=K2 A2l2T-T2

⇒450-TT-0=K2 A2l1 K1 A1l2=9×12×2

⇒450-T=9 T⇒T=45°C

q: A bakelite beaker has volume capacity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cc</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. When it is partially filled with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msub></math> volume (at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>)  of mercury, it is found that the unfilled volume of the beaker remains constant as temperature is varied. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>γ</mi><mtext>bester </mtext></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi></math> is the coefficient of volume expansion, then  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msub></math> (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cc</mi></math> ) is close to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>…</mo></math>
question: A bakelite beaker has volume capacity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cc</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. When it is partially filled with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msub></math> volume (at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>)  of mercury, it is found that the unfilled volume of the beaker remains constant as temperature is varied. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>γ</mi><mtext>bester </mtext></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi></math> is the coefficient of volume expansion, then  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msub></math> (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cc</mi></math> ) is close to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>…</mo></math>
solution:
∆Vm=∆Vc

Vmγm∆T=Vcγc∆T

Vm=Vcγcγm=500×6×10-61.5.×10-4=20cc

subject: Physics
q:
A copper ball of mass 100 g is at a temperature T. It is dropped in a copper calorimeter of mass 100 g, filled with 170 g of water at room temperature. Subsequently, the temperature of the system is found to be 75°C. T is given by:
(Given: room temperature =30°C, specific heat of copper =0.1 cal g-1 °C-1)
question:
A copper ball of mass 100 g is at a temperature T. It is dropped in a copper calorimeter of mass 100 g, filled with 170 g of water at room temperature. Subsequently, the temperature of the system is found to be 75°C. T is given by:
(Given: room temperature =30°C, specific heat of copper =0.1 cal g-1 °C-1)
solution:
mcb=100 g, ToCmc=100 g, 30oCmw=170 g, 30oC

Net heat loss = Net heat gain

⇒100×0.1×T-75=100×0.1×75-30+170×1×75-30

⇒T-75=45+17×45

⇒T=885°C.

q: A metallic sphere cools from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. If atmospheric temperature around is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, then the sphere's temperature after the nest <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> minutes will be close to:
question: A metallic sphere cools from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. If atmospheric temperature around is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, then the sphere's temperature after the nest <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> minutes will be close to:
solution:
Using Newton's Law of cooling

50-405 min=K50+402-20     ......(i)

Next 5 min.

40-θ5=K40+θ2-20     .....(ii)

Dividing (ii)/(i)

40-θ10=40+θ-4050+40-40=θ50

40-θ=θ5

200-5θ=θ

∴   θ=2006=33.3 °C

q: Two rods $A$ and $B$ of identical dimensions are at temperature $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If $\mathrm{A}$ is heated upto $180^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ upto $\mathrm{T}^{\circ} \mathrm{C},$ then the new lengths are the same. If the ratio of the coefficients of linear expansion of $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ is $4: 3,$ then the value of $T$ is
question: Two rods $A$ and $B$ of identical dimensions are at temperature $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If $\mathrm{A}$ is heated upto $180^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ upto $\mathrm{T}^{\circ} \mathrm{C},$ then the new lengths are the same. If the ratio of the coefficients of linear expansion of $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ is $4: 3,$ then the value of $T$ is
solution:
Change in length in both rods are same i.e.

$\Delta \ell_{1}=\Delta \ell_{2}$

$\ell \alpha_{1} \Delta \theta_{1}=\ell \alpha_{2} \Delta \theta_{2}$

$\begin{array}{l}

\frac{\alpha_{1}}{\alpha_{2}}=\frac{\Delta \theta_{2}}{\Delta \theta_{1}} \quad\left[\because \frac{\alpha_{1}}{\alpha_{2}}=\frac{4}{3}\right] \\

\frac{4}{3}=\frac{\theta-30}{180-30}

\end{array}$

$\theta=230^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

.
question:
The graph between two temperature scales P and Q is shown in the figure. Between upper fixed point and lower fixed point there are 150 equal divisions of scale P and 100 divisions on scale Q. The relationship for conversion between the two scales is given by :

.
solution:
From the graph

For scale P,
Lower fixed point=180-150=30°P

Upper fixed point=180°P

For scale Q,
Lower fixed point=0°Q

Upper fixed point=100°Q

Thus, the relationship between the two scales P and Q is given by

tP-30180-30=tQ-0100-0

⇒tP-30150=tQ100

q: A body cools in 7 minutes from 60oC to 40oC. The temperature of the surrounding is 10oC. The temperature of the body after the next 7 minutes will be
question: A body cools in 7 minutes from 60oC to 40oC. The temperature of the surrounding is 10oC. The temperature of the body after the next 7 minutes will be
solution:
Newton's law of cooling states that 

T1-T2t=KT1+T22-T0   ...(i)

where T0 is the temperature of the surrounding. From equation (i),

60-407×60=K60+402-10

⇒207×60=40K⇒K=207×60×40

Let the temperature of the body after the given time be x °C. By Newton's law of cooling,

40-x7×60=K40+x2-10⇒40-x7×60=114×60(20+x2)⇒160-4x=20+x⇒x=28 °C

q: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> grams of steam at <math><msup><mrow><mn>100</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is mixed with <math><mn>200</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of ice at its melting point in a thermally insulated container. If it produces liquid water at <math><msup><mrow><mn>40</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> [heat of vaporization of water is <math><mn>540</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mrow></math> and heat of fusion of ice is <math><mn>80</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mrow></math> ], the value of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> is ________
question: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> grams of steam at <math><msup><mrow><mn>100</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is mixed with <math><mn>200</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of ice at its melting point in a thermally insulated container. If it produces liquid water at <math><msup><mrow><mn>40</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> [heat of vaporization of water is <math><mn>540</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mrow></math> and heat of fusion of ice is <math><mn>80</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mrow></math> ], the value of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> is ________
solution:
MiceLf+Mice40-0Cw=MsteamLv+Msteam100-40Cw
⇒20080+401=M540+601
⇒200120=M600
M=40 gm
